title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: LCFS Credit Prices Decline While Volumes Witness an Uptick WoW
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(1st â€“ 7th December 2025)
  • A total of 538,774 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 364.48% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed an increase of 276.15% WoW as 1,486,007 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $50.51/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 1.87% fall WoW; prices of all non-zero transfers saw a fall of 1.29%, priced at $53.75/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 0.84% WoW; on 9th December, the spot price stood at $52.44/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $51.63/MT.
  • D3 RIN price remained unchanged WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices rose by 2.00%, 3.03% and 2.91% WoW respectively.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Last week, the Clean Fuels Alliance America and the California Advanced Biofuels Alliance (CABA) urged California Air Resources Board (CARB) to update its land-use change (LUC) modeling using the latest science, improved agricultural data, and better economic and carbon models. They emphasized that outdated assumptions could misrepresent the true climate impacts of biofuels.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_608839" align="alignnone" width="837"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer prices recorded a 1.87% WoW decrease, falling from $51.47 to $50.51 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices fell from $54.45 to $53.75, recording a fall of 1.29%. Type 1 trades witnessed 538,774 credits exchanged, recording a 364.48% rise WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw an increase of 276.15% WoW, reaching 1,486,007 credits. [caption id="attachment_608845" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_608846" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices rose by 0.84% WoW, reaching $51.37/MT. On 9th December, the spot price stood at $52.44/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $51.63/MT on 9th December.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_608844" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 78 trades were reported in the market, with 11 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $96.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 10,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $48.50 per credit for a Type 2 transaction reported for 10,000 credits. The largest volume of transfers involved 229,774 credits for a Type 1 transaction, priced at $50.35. The smallest volume of transfers involved 249 credits for a Type 2 transaction priced at $50.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_608843" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $50.51 in December so far, falling from $52.83 in November. As of 6th December, 2025, trade volumes for the month reached 1.49M.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_608842" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 1st â€“7th December 2025, total VPT fell by 1.71% WoW, with a fall in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN price remained unchanged WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices rose by 2.00%, 3.03% and 2.91% WoW respectively. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesÂ 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • Canada Planning Project to Explore Hydrogen Options for Public Transit in the GTHA: Housing, Infrastructure and Communities Canada, alongside the Canadian Hydrogen Association (CHA) and CUTRIC, has announced a combined investment of over $1.25 million to explore hydrogen fueling solutions for public transit in the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Areas (GTHA). Funded primarily through the federal Zero Emission Transit Fund (ZETF) with contributions from the City of Mississauga and CUTRIC partners, the project aims to develop a comprehensive low-carbon fleet transition plan.
  • S. Retail Gasoline Prices Fall Below $3 Per Gallon, the Lowest Since 2021: On December 1, 2025, the U.S. average retail price of regular gasoline fell below $3.00 per gallon (gal) to $2.98/gal, according to data from our Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Update. When adjusted for inflation, the December 1 price is the lowest average U.S. gasoline price since February 2021. The falling price of crude oil, which typically accounts for about half of the retail gasoline price, has led to a drop in the price consumers pay for gasoline. Gasoline prices vary by region. On December 1, regular gasoline prices ranged between a low price of $2.55/gal on the U.S. Gulf Coast and a high price of $4.03/gal on the U.S. West Coast.
  • Governor Newsom Announces $1.1B in Zero-Emission Transit, Safer Roadways, and Resilient Infrastructure: Governor Gavin Newsom announced that the California Transportation Commission (CTC) approved $1.1 billion to fund new zero-emission buses, charging stations, and related infrastructure, as well as investments to restore aging bridges, improve highway safety, and increase mobility on local streets. Guided by the Governorâ€™s Build More, Faster â€“ For All infrastructure agenda, these improvements will make California communities safer and more climate resilient.
  • Clean Fuels Submits Comments to CARB Following Land Use Change Public Forum: Clean Fuels Alliance America, together with California Advanced Biofuels Alliance (CABA), submitted formal comments to CARB after the agencyâ€™s public forum on biofuels and land use change. In their comments, they stressed the need to base land-use change modeling on the most up-to-date science â€” reflecting improvements in agricultural productivity, more accurate economic and carbon models â€” rather than outdated assumptions. â€œOur members represent the majority of the clean diesel fuel used in California today, and we remain committed to working collaboratively with CARB to ensure the LCFS continues to deliver real, near-term emissions reductions,â€ said Cory-Ann Wind, Director of State Regulatory Affairs for Clean Fuels. â€œModernizing land use change modeling is a critical step toward aligning policy with todayâ€™s data and market realities.â€ Clean Fuels and CABA urged CARB to initiate a land use change model modernization effort in 2026, maintain appropriate treatment of waste-based feedstocks and provide clarity for emerging oilseed crops that can deliver additional climate benefits without expanding cropland.
